hook: Your state has a plan for your estate. You should too.
body: Without a will, a court decides who gets your home, your savings, and everything else you've built. That formula is based on state law — not your relationships, not your wishes. A will changes that in a single document.

hook: When a parent passes without a plan, the family pays the price.
body: Probate, family disagreements, frozen accounts — these aren't worst-case scenarios, they're what commonly happens when there's no will or power of attorney in place. The conversation feels awkward, but starting it now is far easier than cleaning up the aftermath later.

hook: A judge shouldn't decide who raises your children. You should.
body: Without a named guardian in your will, a court makes that call if something happens to you. It's one of the hardest parts of estate planning to think about — and one of the most important to actually do. Your kids deserve to land with the right person.

hook: Probate is public, slow, and expensive. It's also avoidable.
body: When an estate goes through probate, a court supervises the whole process — and your family waits. Sometimes for months. The right documents and beneficiary designations can keep most of your estate out of court entirely and get your assets to the right people faster.

hook: Without a power of attorney, you may not be able to help your parent when they need it most.
body: If a parent becomes incapacitated and hasn't signed this document, you could be legally locked out of their finances and healthcare decisions — even as family. Getting that authority through the courts takes time and money. A power of attorney signed now prevents all of it.

hook: Life insurance is only useful if your estate plan is set up to receive it.
body: A policy with no beneficiary named — or one that names a minor child directly — can get tangled in probate or court before your family sees a dollar. Your life insurance and your estate plan need to work together. Most young families haven't checked whether they do.

hook: A trust gives you control a will alone simply cannot.
body: Assets held in a properly funded trust skip probate, transfer faster, and can include conditions a will can't enforce. With tax policy in the news and retirement accounts under scrutiny, more people are revisiting how their estate is structured — and whether a trust belongs in the picture.
